name: LowFat — Aligned-Allocator Pointer Bounds
summary: Software pointer-bounds via aligned size-class allocator: bounds are encoded in the pointer's bit-pattern itself, derived from address alignment. Single-digit % overhead for heap at the cost of no sub-object detection. Research project; no production deployment.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**LowFat** encodes bounds in the pointer's bit-pattern itself via an aligned size-class allocator. The size class — and therefore the bounds — can be recovered from the pointer's high-bit alignment, so no separate metadata table is required.

This is much cheaper than SoftBound for heap allocations (single-digit %) but at the cost of no sub-object bounds detection — bounds are per-allocation only.

Research only; never deployed in production toolchains. Worth naming for design genealogy: the trade-off it makes (cheaper bounds via implicit metadata, no sub-object precision) is the same trade-off MTE makes (cheaper checks via per-granule tags, no sub-object precision).
